original_hypothesis.title: Unlimited approvals in MoeSwapHelper enable router-change risk and token exposure if router is compromised
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: MoeSwapHelper sets token approvals to ROUTER at type(uint).max when allowance is insufficient. If the ROUTER contract is upgraded/compromised or if a malicious router is configured at deployment, it can pull arbitrary token balances from MoeSwapHelper (which temporarily holds user tokens during swaps/refunds).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While ROUTER is immutable, the operational dependency is critical. Any compromise of router control (or deployment misconfiguration) becomes an immediate drain vector for all tokens that pass through the helper.
deployment_assumptions: ROUTER is upgradeable or can be changed in the external protocol governance, or the address configured was incorrect/malicious
MoeSwapHelper can hold non-trivial transient or residual token balances during high volume periods
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: MoeSwapHelper.sol shows immutable ROUTER set in constructor; no repo evidence of how router address is chosen/validated.
affected_functions: swap((uint256 initPosId,uint8 swapType,address tokenIn,address tokenOut,uint256 amtOut,bytes data) _swapInfo)

inventory_refs.function: swap((uint256 initPosId,uint8 swapType,address tokenIn,address tokenOut,uint256 amtOut,bytes data) _swapInfo)

preconditions: ROUTER can move approved funds maliciously (upgrade compromised or malicious deployment address)
MoeSwapHelper holds token balances (during swaps or due to dust/refunds failing for nonstandard tokens)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Per-token infinite approvals are granted to an external router.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after approvals are set

operational_questions: Is the Moe router upgradeable or governed, and what is the upgrade security model?
Do swaps ever leave balances stranded in MoeSwapHelper (fee-on-transfer, blacklisting, etc.)?
Are there monitoring alerts for unexpected transferFrom activity from MoeSwapHelper?
validation_requirements: Confirm ROUTER code and upgradeability on Mantle deployment
Trace allowance-setting events and measure token balances held by helper over time
Simulate router pulling funds using allowance after swap

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait until helper approves router for commonly swapped tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If router is compromised, call transferFrom on those tokens from helper to attacker

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: External dependency compromise cascades into protocol swap flows
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Funds diverted during swaps; users receive incorrect outputs
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium (requires replacing swap helper / router dependency)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All margin swap-based operations via MoeSwapHelper

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpected ERC20 transferFrom events originating from MoeSwapHelper to non-router addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Swap failures correlated with helper balance depletion
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large drains shortly after allowance set

validation_result.mechanism: unlimited approval to immutable trusted external contract
validation_result.impact_summary: The unlimited approval to an immutable ROUTER is a standard gas optimization pattern. MoeSwapHelper holds no residual tokens between transactions, minimizing exposure even if the router were compromised.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis claims that MoeSwapHelper's unlimited approvals to the ROUTER create a drain risk if the router is compromised or misconfigured. While the code does grant type(uint).max approvals, this is not a code defect—it is a deliberate, widely-used gas optimization pattern. The ROUTER address is immutable (set in constructor, never changeable), and MoeSwapHelper is designed to hold zero token balances between swap calls: after each swap, `_refund()` sends all remaining tokenIn back to msg.sender, and swap outputs are directed to msg.sender via the router's recipient parameter. The hypothesis fundamentally describes an external dependency/operational risk (what if the router were compromised externally), not a code-level defect in MoeSwapHelper itself.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: MoeSwapHelper.sol lines 14-45 confirm: (1) ROUTER is immutable (line 15: `address public immutable ROUTER`), (2) _ensureApprove grants type(uint).max approval to ROUTER when current allowance is insufficient (lines 39-42), (3) swap() receives tokens, performs the swap via ROUTER with outputs going to msg.sender, and calls _refund() to return any remaining tokenIn (lines 20-31), (4) _refund() transfers all remaining balance back to msg.sender (lines 33-36). The contract is designed to have zero balance between transactions.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This is the code referenced by the hypothesis. While it does grant unlimited approval, ROUTER is immutable, and the contract holds no tokens between transactions. This is a standard pattern, not a defect.

calling_sequence: 3. _ensureApprove grants type(uint).max approval to ROUTER if needed
4. Router performs the swap, outputs go to msg.sender (MarginTradingHook)
5. _refund() sends any remaining tokenIn back to msg.sender
6. MoeSwapHelper ends with zero token balance
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The full flow shows tokens are only transiently in MoeSwapHelper during a single swap call. After swap+refund, balance returns to zero. The unlimited approval only covers tokens that are already being swapped via the router in the same transaction.

deployment_assumptions_checked: ROUTER is upgradeable or can be changed in the external protocol governance, or the address configured was incorrect/malicious
MoeSwapHelper can hold non-trivial transient or residual token balances during high volume periods
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. However, the code itself proves ROUTER is immutable (set once in constructor). The _refund() function ensures no residual balances remain after swap execution.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario requires: (1) ROUTER being compromised or malicious—but ROUTER is immutable and cannot be changed after deployment; (2) MoeSwapHelper holding non-trivial token balances—but _refund() returns all remaining tokens after each swap, and swap outputs go directly to msg.sender via the router recipient parameter. The only window where tokens exist in MoeSwapHelper is during an active swap() call, which is atomic. A compromised router during an active swap could misdirect outputs, but that's an external protocol risk, not a MoeSwapHelper code defect. The unlimited approval doesn't create additional exposure because the contract holds no tokens at rest.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No code-level impact. The unlimited approval pattern is a standard gas optimization. The contract's design ensures zero residual token balances between calls. The hypothesis describes an external dependency risk (router compromise), which is an operational concern that applies equally to exact-amount approvals—a compromised router could steal tokens during the swap itself regardless of approval size.
recommended_mitigations: Consider approving only the exact amount needed before each swap and resetting to 0 after (defense-in-depth, at higher gas cost)
Document the trust assumption on ROUTER in code comments
Monitor ROUTER address for any governance/upgrade activity in the external Moe protocol
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy MoeSwapHelper with a mock router, execute a swap, verify contract balance is zero after swap completes, verify approval is type(uint).max to router, confirm no tokens can be pulled when contract balance is zero.
uncertainties: Whether the Moe Router contract is upgradeable or behind a proxy is unknown from the provided code
Whether there are edge cases with fee-on-transfer tokens that could leave dust in MoeSwapHelper
additional_testing_needed: Verify Moe Router upgradeability on Mantle deployment
Test with fee-on-transfer tokens to check for residual dust after _refund()
Check if safeApprove would revert for tokens requiring zero-first approval when partial allowance remains (separate issue from hypothesis)
